This chapter blueprints the (currently-missing) Lean file

**invertible sheaf `O_C(D)`** for a smooth proper geometrically
irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a Weil divisor `D`. The Lean file will
be opened in iter-183 as a file-skeleton lane; this chapter sets up
the blueprint so the HARD GATE clears.

  typed-sorry `noncomputable def` with no chapter pinning. Multiple
  downstream chapters consume it:
  - `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex` — the χ-identity inductive proof
    consumes `sheafOf`'s body for both base case and inductive step
    (iter-181 Lane H landed 2 helper sorries gated on this).
  - `RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` is the
    `D = [P]` specialisation of `sheafOf` (per the iter-182 analogist
    consult `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom`, the project's most viable path
    is Hartshorne's subsheaf-of-`K_C` direct construction).
  - `RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex` — Pin 2/Pin 3 both consume
    `sheafOf` via the function-field/Weil-divisor degree bridge.

- **Out of scope**: actually creating the Lean file (iter-183 work).
  This chapter is statement-level for the file's eventual top decls.

## Chapter outline

Sections to write:

### §1 — Background and motivation (~30-50 lines)

The setting: smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over
algebraically closed field `k̄`; the Picard group; line bundles vs
Weil divisors (correspondence via Hartshorne II.6.18).

### §2 — Definition of `O_C(D)` (~80-120 lines)

Pin the load-bearing definition:

```latex
\begin{definition}[Invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$]
  \label{def:sheafOf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
  \uses{def:scheme_weil_divisor, def:scheme_function_field}
  % SOURCE: Hartshorne, II.6, "Cartier divisors", II.6.18
  % SOURCE QUOTE: <verbatim text from references/hartshorne-graduate.pdf>
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6.}
  For a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_Q [Q]$ on a smooth curve $C / \bar k$,
  the sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is the subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$
  (the constant sheaf at the function field) given by
  $$\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) = \{ f \in K(C) : \text{ord}_Q(f) \geq -n_Q
    \text{ for all prime divisors } Q \in U \}.$$
\end{definition}
```

Three immediate corollaries (all blueprint-pinned):

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Sheaf of $0$ is structure sheaf]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
  $\mathcal O_C(0) = \mathcal O_C$ canonically as sheaves on $C$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Sheaf at a closed point is the iter-181 lineBundleAtClosedPoint]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
  For a closed point $P$ on $C$,
  $\mathcal O_C([P]) = \text{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}$
  (the `OCofP.lean` construction).
\end{lemma>

\begin{lemma}[SES additivity]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_additivity}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
  For any prime divisor $Y$ and any $n : \mathbb Z$:
  $$0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{point at } Y]} \to 0$$
  is a short exact sequence (where the rightmost is the skyscraper sheaf at $Y$).
\end{lemma}
```

### §3 — Proof of correctness (~60-100 lines)

Sketch the construction at the presheaf level + the sheaf property:

1. **Presheaf**: $U \mapsto \{ f \in K(C) : \text{ord}_Q(f) \geq -n_Q
   \forall Q \in U \}$ with restriction by identity on $K(C)$.
2. **Sheaf property**: the order conditions are LOCAL at each prime
   divisor; gluing reduces to the local-ring property at each prime
   divisor's stalk (a DVR for a smooth curve over $\bar k$).
3. **Invertibility**: locally on `U \setminus \{Q\}`, the constraint
   on `ord_Q` vanishes; globally `O_C(D)` is locally free of rank 1.

### §4 — `\lean{...}` pins (~10-15 lines)

Each load-bearing declaration above carries a `\lean{...}` pin
naming the eventual top-level declaration in `OcOfD.lean`. The Lean
file's iter-183 file-skeleton lane will create exactly these
declarations as typed sorries.

3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.

108	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,

236	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}
239	
240	
241	\leanok
242	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
243	controlled pole]
244	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
246	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
247	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField,
248	        def:order_at_point,
249	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
250	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
251	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
252	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
253	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
254	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
255	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
257	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
258	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
259	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
260	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
261	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
262	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
263	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
264	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
265	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
266	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
268	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
269	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
270	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
271	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
272	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
273	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
274	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
275	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
276	  Riemann--Roch space
277	  \[
278	    L([P]) \;:=\;
279	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
280	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
281	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
282	  \]
283	  Equivalently, the order conditions
284	  \[
285	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
286	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
287	    \qquad
288	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
289	  \]
290	  on $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ are equivalent to the existence of a global
291	  section $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ whose image $\iota(s)$ under
292	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
293	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ equals $f$ (the iff binds $s$ to $f$
294	  explicitly — vacuous-in-$f$ readings such as ``some nonzero global
295	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
296	
297	  Under this identification, the inclusion
298	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
299	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
300	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
301	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
302	\end{lemma}
303	
304	\begin{proof}
305	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
306	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
307	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
308	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
309	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
310	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
311	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
312	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
313	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
314	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
315	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
316	  and only if:
317	
318	  \begin{itemize}
319	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
320	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
321	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
322	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
323	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
324	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
325	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
326	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
327	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
328	  \end{itemize}
329	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
330	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
331	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
332	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
333	  zero global section.
334	
335	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
336	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
337	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
338	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
339	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
340	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
341	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
342	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
343	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
344	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
345	\end{proof}

347	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
348	
349	\begin{lemma}
350	
351	
352	\leanok
353	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
354	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
355	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
356	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
357	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
358	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
359	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
360	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
361	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
362	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
363	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
364	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
365	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
366	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
367	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
368	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
369	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
370	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
371	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
372	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
373	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
374	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
375	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
376	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
377	  \[
378	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
379	  \]
380	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
385	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
386	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
387	  exact sequence
388	  \[
389	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
390	  \]
391	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
392	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
393	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
394	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
395	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
396	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
397	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
398	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
399	  \[
400	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
401	  \]
402	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
403	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
404	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
405	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
406	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
407	  \begin{multline*}
408	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
409	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
410	  \end{multline*}
411	  Two ingredients close the proof:
412	
413	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
414	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
415	  \[
416	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
417	  \]
418	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
419	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
420	
421	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
422	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
423	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
424	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
425	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
426	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
427	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
428	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
429	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
430	
431	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
432	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
433	  \[
434	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
435	  \]
436	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
437	
438	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
439	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
440	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
441	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
442	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
443	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
444	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
445	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
446	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
447	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
448	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
449	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
450	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
451	  complex degenerates).
452	\end{proof}

502	  genus hypothesis $g(C) = 0$ (\Cref{def:genus}) gives
503	  \[
504	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
505	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
506	  \]
507	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} as a two-term Euler
508	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
509	  \[
510	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
511	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
512	    \;=\; 2.
513	  \]
514	  By
515	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}, the second
516	  term vanishes:
517	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$. Substituting yields
518	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, as required.
519	
520	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
521	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
522	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} computes
523	  the dimension of $H^0$ in one stroke without invoking
524	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
525	  \[
526	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
527	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
528	  \]
529	  together with $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$
530	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: $H^0$ of the structure sheaf on a proper
531	  geometrically irreducible variety over $\bar k$ is $\bar k$),
532	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ (skyscraper at a $\bar k$-rational
533	  point), and $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ (genus-$0$), the connecting
534	  map $H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ has zero codomain and the
535	  segment yields the short exact sequence
536	  \[
537	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
538	  \]
539	  of $\bar k$-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
540	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
541	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
542	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
543	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
544	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
545	\end{proof}

547	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
548	
549	\begin{corollary}
550	
551	
552	\leanok
553	[A genus-$0$ curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
554	single simple pole]
555	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
556	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
557	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
558	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
560	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
561	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
562	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
563	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
564	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
565	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
566	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
567	  % point on a genus-$0$ curve, as a direct consequence of the
568	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
569	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
570	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let $X$ be a curve, and let $P \in X$ be a
571	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
572	  % K(X)$, which is regular everywhere except at $P$."
573	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
574	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
575	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
576	  there exists a non-constant rational function $f \in K(C)$ such that:
577	  \begin{itemize}
578	    \item $f$ is regular on $C \setminus \{P\}$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
579	      for every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$;
580	    \item $f$ has at most a simple pole at $P$, i.e.\
581	      $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$;
582	    \item $f \notin \bar k$, equivalently
583	      $\div(f) \neq 0 \in \Div(C)$.
584	  \end{itemize}
585	  In particular, the pair $(1, f)$ spans
586	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
587	\end{corollary}
588	
589	\begin{proof}
590	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
591	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
592	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
593	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
594	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
595	  By
596	  \Cref{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero},
597	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$. The constant subspace
598	  $\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, obtained from the
599	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
600	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
601	  $f = 1$, with $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, under the
602	  identification of
603	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}), is a one-dimensional
604	  $\bar k$-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension $2$,
605	  the quotient $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1$ is a
606	  non-zero one-dimensional $\bar k$-vector space; any lift to
607	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of a non-zero element of the quotient is
608	  an element of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1$.
609	
610	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
611	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
612	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} the section
613	  $s$ corresponds to a rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$
614	  satisfying $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, equivalently $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
615	  for every $Q \neq P$ and $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$. The choice
616	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ means $f$ is not the image of a constant,
617	  so $f \notin \bar k$ (every non-zero element of
618	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k$ corresponds, under the
619	  identification, to a constant element of $K(C)$). The three bullets
620	  of the statement are now immediate.
621	
622	  Spanning. Linear independence of $1$ and $f$ over $\bar k$ in
623	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ is the same condition as
624	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ above; combined with the dimension count
625	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, the pair $(1, f)$ is a
626	  $\bar k$-basis of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$.
627	
628	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
629	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
630	  returns an element $f$ of the project's function-field carrier
631	  $K(C)$ together with proofs of regularity on $C \setminus \{P\}$ and
632	  the simple-pole bound at $P$, packaged in the same shape as
633	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}'s output. The
634	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
635	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
636	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
637	\end{proof}

639	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H$^1$-vanishing input}
640	
641	The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve
642	(\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}) is the
643	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
644	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
645	elsewhere:
646	
647	\begin{itemize}
648	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of $\Module \bar k$-flavoured sheaf
649	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
650	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
651	    $\Ext^*$-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
652	    project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured pipeline
653	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
654	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
655	    consumed by \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of
656	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
657	    obligation.
658	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
659	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
660	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
661	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC} and surrounding
662	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
663	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
664	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
665	    $\chi(k(P)) = 1$, which is the combined statement
666	    $\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ and $\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$,
667	    so the vanishing $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ is already a consumer
668	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
669	\end{itemize}
670	
671	Consequently the H$^1$-vanishing
672	\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} introduces no
673	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
674	path. The genus-$0$ specialisation should therefore close at the
675	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
676	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.

12	## Question
13	
14	`RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean` carries two load-bearing `noncomputable def := sorry`
15	declarations:
16	
17	- `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` (L140) returning a
18	  `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
19	- `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` (L154) returning a `kbar`-linear
20	  map from sections (`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 0`) to
21	  `C.left.functionField`.
22	
23	iter-181 lean-auditor MAJOR finding: until `toFunctionField`'s body lands, the
24	proven `globalSections_iff` is mathematically vacuous (the RHS quantifies over
25	`toFunctionField P hP s = f` where `toFunctionField` is `sorryAx`).
26	
27	The directive asks: what is the canonical Mathlib idiom, what gaps exist, and
28	should the project (a) build the bodies inline in `OCofP.lean`, (b) open a new
29	`IdealSheafDual.lean` bottom-up file, or (c) `NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL`?

43	## Decisions identified
44	
45	### Decision 1 — Sheaf-internal-Hom for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat R)`-valued sheaves
46	
47	- **Mathlib idiom**: There is **no** internal-Hom for sheaves of `ModuleCat R`
48	  with the value `Sheaf J (ModuleCat R)`. Mathlib only exposes:
49	  - `CategoryTheory.sheafHom : Sheaf J A → Sheaf J A → Sheaf J (Type _)`
50	    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafHom`, L42-L100) — wrong codomain
51	    (Type, not `ModuleCat R`).
52	  - `SheafOfModules.Hom : SheafOfModules R → SheafOfModules R → Type _`
53	    (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf`) — the *external* Hom (a
54	    bare type), not a sheaf of `R`-modules.
55	  - The category `X.Modules := SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf`
56	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf` L37) is the abelian category of
57	    `O_X`-modules, but `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf.lean` defines
58	    NO internal-Hom / dual functor. The file has only pushforward / pullback /
59	    restriction. Verified via
60	    `grep -rn "internalHom\|^def ihom\|sheafHom\|dual" .lake/.../Modules/`.
61	- **Project's current path**: typed `sorry` body, with iter-180 Lane D
62	  task_result naming this exact gap.
63	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost — the Hartshorne "Hom_{O_C}(I_P, O_C)" entry
64	  point through Mathlib's `IdealSheafData` is **structurally blocked** on
65	  three layers: (i) `IdealSheafData` is "data describing an ideal sheaf",
66	  not yet a `SheafOfModules` (Mathlib's own docstring at
67	  `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic` L38 says: "Ideal sheaves are
68	  not yet defined in this file as actual subsheaves of `O_X`"); (ii) no
69	  Mathlib `IdealSheafData → SheafOfModules` realiser exists; (iii) even if
70	  (i)+(ii) existed, no `SheafOfModules → SheafOfModules` dual functor exists.
71	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL for the "Hom_{O_C}(I_P, O_C)" path.

73	### Decision 2 — Ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point
74	
75	- **Mathlib idiom**:
76	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal :
77	    TopologicalSpace.Closeds X → X.IdealSheafData`
78	  (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic`, L? per `lean_leansearch`).
79	  Packages `{P}` as a closed subset → an `IdealSheafData` recording the
80	  ideal-component over each affine open.
81	- **Project's current path**: docstring at OCofP L131-L134 mentions
82	  `IdealSheafData.idealOfPoint`, but **that exact name does not exist** in
83	  Mathlib (`lean_local_search "idealOfPoint"` returns no Mathlib hits — the
84	  correct construction is `vanishingIdeal ⟨{P}, hP⟩`, with `hP : IsClosed`
85	  packaged into `Closeds`).

87	  function exists); divergent-with-cost at the sheaf level (no
88	  `IdealSheafData → SheafOfModules` realiser, see Decision 1).
89	- **Verdict**: PROCEED at the data level (use `vanishingIdeal`),
90	  NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL at the sheaf level.

92	### Decision 3 — Subsheaf-of-the-function-field (Hartshorne's alternative)
93	
94	Hartshorne II §6 p. 144 packages `O_C(D)` DIRECTLY as a subsheaf of the
95	function-field constant sheaf `K_C`. For `D = [P]` on a curve, sections over
96	`U` are rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` with order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0`
97	for every prime divisor `Q ∈ U`, `Q ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U`.
98	
99	- **Mathlib idiom** *(closest analogue)*:
100	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.functionField`
101	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField`) — `K(C)` as a `Field`-typed
102	    `CommRingCat`, for `IrreducibleSpace X` (auto on `IsIntegral`).
103	  - `CategoryTheory.Subpresheaf` / `Subfunctor`
104	    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Subsheaf`) — but it's **`Type _`-valued
105	    only**, not `ModuleCat R`-valued. So Mathlib has no off-the-shelf
106	    "subsheaf of `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaf" gadget.
107	  - However, the **project itself already has the recipe** for landing a
108	    `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat kbar)`
109	    bottom-up: `Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf` + `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf`
110	    (`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/{Presheaf,SheafProperty}.lean`)
111	    builds `O_C` as a `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` by hand from
112	    `C.left.presheaf` and the `Spec kbar`-algebra structure. The same
113	    template applies to `O_C(P)`: per-open submodule of `K(C)`, restriction
114	    is identity-on-`K(C)`, sheaf property reduces to order-conditions
115	    being local at prime divisors.
116	- **Project's current path**: not used yet (Decision 1's Hartshorne-II.6
117	  "Hom_{O_C}(I_P, O_C)" alternative description was the intended path; the
118	  subsheaf-of-`K_C` description is the project's bypass).
119	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent — both descriptions yield the same invertible
120	  sheaf (Hartshorne II.6.18 / Proposition 6.13(a)); the subsheaf-of-`K_C`
121	  description sidesteps both Mathlib gaps from Decision 1.
122	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on the recipe (mirror `toModuleKSheaf`'s
123	  presheaf-by-hand pattern), DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY on the abstract

157	```lean
158	-- 1. The subset of K(C) with controlled pole at P, on an open U.
159	private def lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrier
160	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
161	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
162	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
163	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
164	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) (U : (Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
165	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField :=
166	  { carrier := { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 →
167	                  Q.point ≠ P → 0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
168	                  (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤
169	                    Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f) }
170	    zero_mem' := by … (order of 0 is +∞)
171	    add_mem' := by … (order is super-additive / non-archimedean)
172	    smul_mem' := by … (k̄-scalar doesn't change orders at prime divisors) }
173	
174	-- 2. Presheaf: restriction is identity-on-K(C), constraint set shrinks.
175	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint_presheaf … :
176	    (Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar where
177	  obj U := ModuleCat.of kbar ↥(lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrier P hPcoh U)
178	  map {U V} f := ModuleCat.ofHom
179	    -- inclusion from larger constraint set (V ⊆ U means constraint on V ⊆ constraint on U)
180	    (Submodule.inclusion (by
181	      intro x hx
182	      refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => hx.1 Q (Set.mem_of_subset_of_mem ?leOfHom hQU) hQP, ?_⟩
183	      · intro hPV
184	        exact hx.2 (Set.mem_of_subset_of_mem ?leOfHom hPV)))
185	  map_id := …
186	  map_comp := …
187	
188	-- 3. Sheaf property: order conditions at a prime divisor Q are local at Q's point;
189	--    cover U by Vᵢ; if f satisfies conditions on each Vᵢ then it satisfies on U.
190	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint_presheaf_isSheaf … :
191	    Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
192	      (lineBundleAtClosedPoint_presheaf P hPcoh) := by
193	  -- Two-step: first show the FORGET to Type is a sheaf (via subobject-of-constant-sheaf-is-sheaf),
194	  -- then apply Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget on (CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat kbar)).
195	  -- Mathematical core: the underlying set-valued presheaf is a subpresheaf of the constant
196	  -- presheaf at K(C); each constraint "ord_Q(f) ≥ k" is a stalk-local condition at Q.
197	  sorry  -- ~100 LOC of "order conditions glue" lemmas
198	
199	-- 4. Bundle as Sheaf
200	noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint … : Sheaf … (ModuleCat kbar) :=
201	  ⟨lineBundleAtClosedPoint_presheaf P hPcoh,
202	   lineBundleAtClosedPoint_presheaf_isSheaf P hPcoh⟩
203	```

3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),

12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[

% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean

\chapter{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor (RR.2$_\ast$)}
\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}

% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
% (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$; Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
% sequence). Read directly from
% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
% (the sheaf $\mathcal O_X(D)$ from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); together with \texttt{RR.1}
(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}), \texttt{RR.3}
(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}), and \texttt{RR.4}
(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
\[
  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
  \,\colon\,
  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
\]
The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
functor
\[
  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \Div(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
\]
sending a Weil divisor $D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]$ to its associated invertible
sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ on $C$. The Lean entry-point
\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
three immediate corollaries will live.

\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
$\chi$-identity proof of \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor $D$ via the
``$D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
short exact sequence
\[
  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
  \to 0
\]
to be exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, which is not a statement on the
\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
landed two helper sorries
(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
much $\chi$-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.

\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}) constructs
the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$ associated to a \emph{closed point}
$P \in C$ directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below makes this bypass mathematically
coherent by exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(D)$ at $D = [P]$ as canonically
isomorphic to \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. With this isomorphism in
hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via the
$\chi$-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.

\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
language of $C$ both reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ for general $D$ (not
just $D = [P]$): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
$D \in \Div(C)$ and reads off $\deg(D)$ from the
\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
\Cref{def:sheafOf}).

\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
\begin{quote}
  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
\end{quote}
The Weil divisor group $\Div(C)$ is the one defined in
\Cref{def:codim1_cycles} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
prime-divisor data type is \Cref{def:prime_divisor}; the order
$\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor $Q$ is \Cref{def:order_at_point}; the
principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a non-zero rational function is
\Cref{def:principal_divisor}; the closed-point divisor $[P]$ is
\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The line bundle at a closed point
already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, and the present chapter's
\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} makes the comparison
$\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ explicit.

\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$:
(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ (rejected:
no \texttt{IdealSheafData $\to$ SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
no $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ dual functor in Mathlib);
(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$} description (accepted).
The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
\texttt{sheafOf $D$} as well, and \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} verifies
that the general construction at $D = [P]$ recovers the closed-point
specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$
recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
$\mathscr L(D)$) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
project's choice to use it both at the general $D$ and at the $D = [P]$
specialisation is what makes the comparison
$\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ a direct unfolding
rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.

\section{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$}

\begin{definition}[Invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor]
  \label{def:sheafOf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor, def:order_at_point,
        def:principal_divisor}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
  % (Cartier $\leftrightarrow$ Weil correspondence on an integral
  % locally factorial scheme; identification of $\mathscr L(D)$ as a
  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field $K$). Read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, so $f_i^{-1}$ and
  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If $X$ is an integral
  % scheme, the homomorphism $\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X$ of
  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of $\mathscr K$, which
  % in this case is the constant sheaf $K$, where $K$ is the function
  % field of $X$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on $X$ and
  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes $Y$, i.e., subschemes
  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
  % Note also that if $X$ is an integral separated noetherian locally
  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
  $\mathscr L(D)$), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-$K$
  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  $\bar k$ and let $D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor
  on $C$, with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors $Q$ of $C$ and
  supported on a finite set $\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}$
  (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The \emph{invertible sheaf
  $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$}, also written
  $\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)$ in Hartshorne's notation, is the
  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the constant sheaf
  $\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
  field) defined section-wise by
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
  \]
  for each open $U \subseteq C$. Restriction along $V \hookrightarrow U$
  is the identity on the underlying carrier $K(C)$, which preserves
  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
  the constraint set on $U$ embeds into the constraint set on $V$). The
  $\bar k$-module structure on
  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is restricted from the
  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$ (a $\bar k$-scalar multiple of a
  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).

  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
  formal sum $D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]$ packages, on a curve where prime
  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
  divisors are DVRs (smoothness $+$ dimension~$1$), the Cartier-divisor
  data $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
  open $U_i$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ in the support of
  $D$, pick a uniformiser $\pi_Q \in K(C)$ at $Q$ and set
  $f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}$; on opens disjoint from the support of $D$, set
  $f_i := 1$. The transitions on overlaps $U_i \cap U_j$ are units in
  $\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}$ for each prime $Q$ in the overlap (any
  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
  $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original $D$. The
  ``$f_i^{-1}$-generated'' subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$ from Hartshorne's
  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
  the local generator $f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}$ has order $-n_Q$ at $Q$,
  so the local sections are exactly $f \in K(C)$ with
  $\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$, on
  $U_i$.

  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP})
  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
  \mathcal K_C$ is an invertible sheaf on $C$, independently of the
  choice of uniformisers $\pi_Q$ (any two choices differ by a unit, so
  they generate the same $\mathcal O_C$-submodule).

  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
  divisor $D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}$ and returns a
  $\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ on
  $C.\text{left.toTopCat}$, where
  $J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}$ is the
  standard topology on the topological space underlying $C$. The
  closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$ is
  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of
  \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}; their agreement at $D = [P]$ is
  \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below. The construction follows
  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
  $\bar k$-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
\end{definition}

\section{Immediate corollaries}

\begin{lemma}[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  $\bar k$. Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
  $0 \in \Div(C)$ is canonically the structure sheaf:
  \[
    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
  \]
  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
  At $D = 0$ the coefficient $n_Q = 0$ at every prime divisor $Q$, so the
  section condition of \Cref{def:sheafOf} reduces, on every open
  $U \subseteq C$, to
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
  \]
  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
  scheme $C$ (the regular functions on $U$ are exactly the rational
  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
  $C$ landing in $U$; this is the standard ``non-negative order $=$
  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$. The restriction maps on both sides are
  the identity on $K(C)$, so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
  equality of sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
        def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
  $[P] \in \Div(C)$ via \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. Then the
  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ of \Cref{def:sheafOf} agrees on
  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ of \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}:
  \[
    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
  \]
  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point}
  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
  $[P]$ has coefficients $n_Q = 1$ if $Q = P$ and $n_Q = 0$ otherwise
  (where $P$ is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Thus
  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ contains $0$ and those
  $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ satisfying $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime
  divisor $Q \in U$ with $Q \neq P$, plus $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$ if
  $P \in U$.

  On the \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} side, the same conditions
  are exactly the ones extracted by
  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} of
  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open $U$ (not only at the global section
  $U = C$): a rational function $f \in K(C)$ is a section of
  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ on $U$ iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
  $Q \in U \setminus \{P\}$ and has at most a simple pole at $P$ when
  $P \in U$.

  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on $K(C)$. The two
  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ are therefore equal
  section-by-section as subsets of $K(C)$, and the equality propagates
  to an equality of sheaves of $\mathcal O_C$-modules. The
  $\bar k$-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$, so the identification is
  $\bar k$-linear on each section.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Short exact sequence for $D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$]
  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
        def:divisor_closed_point}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any
  % point...''). Read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$.
  % (Since $\mathscr L(D + P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
  % it does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$.)"
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  $\bar k$, let $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor, and let $P \in C$ be
  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
  \Cref{def:prime_divisor} on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ of
  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ fits into a short exact
  sequence
  \[
    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
  \]
  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (equivalently, in
  $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$), where $k(P)$ denotes the
  skyscraper sheaf at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$ (the residue field of $P$
  is $\bar k$ since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $P$ is a
  $\bar k$-rational closed point).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
        lem:sheafOf_singlePoint, def:order_at_point}
  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
  proceeds in two beats:

  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at $P$).} The closed-subscheme
  inclusion $P \hookrightarrow C$ has structure sheaf
  $\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)$ (a skyscraper at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$),
  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
  $\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])$ (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
  $\mathcal K_C$ generated by a local uniformiser $\pi_P$ at $P$ and by
  $1$ away from $P$, i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
  principal closed subscheme $P$). This produces the structural short
  exact sequence
  \[
    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
  \]
  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$.

  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$).} The line bundle
  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is locally free of rank~$1$
  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), \Cref{def:sheafOf}), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is exact on $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
  \[
    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
  \]
  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
      $\Div(C)$-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
      ring-theoretic identity
      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
      rational functions, where $n_Q^{D}$ is the coefficient of $Q$
      in $D$);
    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
    \item $k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)$ (tensoring a
      skyscraper at $P$ by a locally free rank-$1$ sheaf preserves the
      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over $P$ the line bundle
      $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P$ is a free $\mathcal O_{C,P}$-module of
      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
      \cong k(P)$ is just $k(P)$; away from $P$ the skyscraper is the
      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
  \end{itemize}
  Substituting yields
  \[
    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
  \]
  exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, as required.

  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is the
  inclusion of subsheaves of $\mathcal K_C$: at the level of sections,
  the constraint $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ of
  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ implies the weaker constraint
  $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1$ of $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
  when $P \in U$ (constraints on $Q \neq P$ are unchanged), and the map
  is identity on $K(C)$ at the carrier level. The second map
  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)$ is the evaluation
  ``leading coefficient at the pole at $P$'' (i.e.\ the
  $\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}$-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of $f$
  at $P$), which is well-defined on $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
  modulo the subgroup of sections with $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ ---
  i.e.\ modulo $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
\end{proof}

\section{Sheaf-property correctness}

The construction of \Cref{def:sheafOf} is presheaf-by-hand: a
per-open $\bar k$-submodule of $K(C)$, with restriction along
$V \hookrightarrow U$ given by the identity on $K(C)$. This section
sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
target category $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.

\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For $V \subseteq U$, the
constraint set defining $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is a \emph{subset}
of the constraint set defining $\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$ (fewer
prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on $K(C)$ restricts
to a well-defined inclusion
$\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
Composition $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$ is again identity-on-$K(C)$, so
strict functoriality holds.

\paragraph{$\bar k$-linearity.} A $\bar k$-scalar $\lambda \in \bar k$
acts on $K(C)$ by multiplication, and
$\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)$ for $\lambda \neq 0$
(units have order zero); for $\lambda = 0$, $\lambda \cdot f = 0$ is
included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
$\bar k$-submodule and the restriction maps are $\bar k$-linear.

\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
``$\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$'' is
\emph{stalk-local} at each $Q$: it depends only on the germ of $f$ at
the point of $C$ underlying $Q$, equivalently on the image of $f$ in
the DVR $\mathcal O_{C, Q}$. Consequently the sheaf property reduces
to two ingredients:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is a
    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at $K(C)$.} The constant
    presheaf at $K(C)$ is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
    prime divisor.
  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ to
    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})$.} The categorical forget
    $\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}$ creates limits, hence
    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of $\bar k$-modules is a
    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
    this delivers the sheaf property for the
    $\bar k$-module-valued $\mathcal O_C(D)$.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open $U \subseteq C$ disjoint from
the support of $D$, the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
constraint $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$; hence
$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U$, and the trivialisation
$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U$ is the identity. On a small
open $U \subseteq C$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ from the
support of $D$, the local generator $\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)$ trivialises
$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U$ as a free rank-$1$ $\mathcal O_C|_U$-module: a
section $f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
-n_Q$ iff $\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
``multiplication by $\pi_Q^{n_Q}$'' map is an
$\mathcal O_C|_U$-linear isomorphism
$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U$. The two types
of trivialising opens cover $C$ (any prime divisor lies in at most
finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of $D$ is
finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
the cocycle is in $\mathcal O_C^{\times}$ and the sheaf is invertible.

The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
landed the analogous bottom-up
$\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$-valued construction for the
structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
\texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{def:order_at_point} and surrounding lemmas).
